target.title: fix(opencode): restore Zen model catalog
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the bundled `opencode` Zen provider exposed auth/setup but did not own a Zen model catalog/resolver, so users had to hand-register `opencode/<model>` rows; review also found route/cost/docs drift around MiniMax, static Zen metadata, and the documented Gemini example.
- Solution: restore the Zen provider-owned catalog, dynamic resolver, runtime discovery filter, route normalization, provider-discovery static catalog, manifest anchor metadata, cost metadata, and regression tests at the `opencode` plugin boundary.
- Latest follow-up: this update moves the full 49-row offline Zen catalog behind `providerCatalogEntry`/provider discovery, trims `openclaw.plugin.json` to four representative route/cost anchors, keeps the documented Gemini and GLM-5.2 examples resolvable, and adds a sync-main follow-up regression for credential-scoped live discovery caching.
- What changed: the PR now filters live discovery to provider-owned rows only, keeps the curated 49-row seed visible through provider discovery when live discovery is unavailable, routes MiniMax through the Zen OpenAI-compatible `/v1/chat/completions` path, preserves required `cost` metadata for every runtime row and manifest anchor, verifies the docs examples are resolvable, proves different OpenCode discovery credentials do not share cached live catalog rows, and adds `glm-5.2` after authenticated Zen `/models` returned it.
- **Why it matters / User impact:** users can select advertised `opencode/<model>` Zen models without hand-writing every model row, and docs no longer point them at an unresolved Gemini reference.
- **Out of scope:** this does not change non-OpenCode providers, `opencode-go` runtime behavior, generic LLM adapter APIs, the shared OpenCode credential fallback policy, or the public model/config cost contract.
- Fixes #92479

## Origin / follow-up

- **Follows / completes:** This updates the existing #92495 repair branch after ClawSweeper flagged live-only Zen row synthesis, missing successful MiniMax live proof, cost-contract failures, and a stale documented Gemini example.
- **Gap this fills:** The branch now proves the documented examples resolve, refreshes authenticated Zen `/models` proof with a redacted key, and keeps MiniMax on the verified OpenAI-compatible Zen route.
- **Consistency:** The live-discovery behavior matches the shared `buildLiveModelProviderConfig` shape used by `opencode-go`: use live discovery when authenticated rows are available, filter to provider-owned metadata rows, and retain the static seed when discovery is unavailable or not provider-owned. The offline static surface also now mirrors `opencode-go` by exposing the complete catalog through provider discovery instead of forcing every row into the manifest.

## Competition / linked PR analysis

- **Linked PR(s):** #92495 is the existing linked PR for #92479; `check-upstream-fixed` found `origin/main` does not already cover this repair.
- **Related open PR / same-contract scan:** The linked issue and ClawSweeper review identify this PR as the active candidate fix. I did not find a same-contract canonical PR on `origin/main` superseding the Zen catalog owner-boundary repair.
- **Gap in linked PR(s):** The current linked PR itself had author-actionable gaps: live-only discovery rows were exposed with inferred metadata, MiniMax was grouped with the Zen Anthropic Messages route, cost metadata briefly violated the required model contract, and docs referenced `gemini-3-pro`, which the provider could not resolve.
- **Why this patch is better/canonical:** It fixes the gaps at the provider-owned catalog/routing/docs boundary instead of adding downstream special cases, publishing guessed live-only rows, or widening the shared model schema from one provider PR.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The bundled `opencode` Zen provider now exposes usable `opencode/<model>` rows without hand-written user config. Documented Zen examples resolve through the provider runtime, authenticated Zen `/models` discovery returns the expected curated rows including `glm-5.2`, unknown live-only IDs are not promoted without owned metadata, MiniMax uses the OpenAI-compatible Zen v1 route, and every static Zen row remains compatible with the required model cost contract.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux, Node/Vitest/pnpm in the local OpenClaw PR worktree. The OpenCode Zen API key was used only as a transient environment variable for live proof; logs redact secret-like strings and the key is not stored in the repo or PR body.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd "$TASK_WORKTREE"

git diff --check
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/opencode/index.test.ts

# Fresh validation after sync-main and the credential-scoped cache regression:
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/opencode/index.test.ts
git diff --check

pnpm docs:list
pnpm run check:test-types
pnpm run tsgo:prod
pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:compile
pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:canary

OPENCODE_API_KEY=[REDACTED] node --import tsx <redacted live proof runner>
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer [REDACTED]" -H "Accept: application/json" https://opencode.ai/zen/v1/models
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Focused provider regression tests now include the docs/catalog contract, provider-discovery offline catalog contract, manifest-anchor cost contract, live-only discovery filtering, static fallback, and credential-scoped live discovery cache behavior. The earlier branch validation passed all 13 tests, and the fresh sync-main follow-up passes all 14 tests:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  13 passed (13)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 56.04s
```

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/opencode/index.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  14 passed (14)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 29.99s
```

```text
git diff --check
# passed with no output
```

The docs list command completed before the type/package-boundary gates, and the type gates completed successfully:

```text
## pnpm docs:list
$ node scripts/docs-list.js
Listing all markdown files in docs folder:
...
## pnpm run check:test-types
$ pnpm tsgo:test
...
## pnpm run tsgo:prod
$ pnpm tsgo:core && pnpm tsgo:extensions
```

The extension package-boundary checks passed in both compile and canary modes:

```text
extension package boundary check passed
mode: compile
compiled plugins: 115
canary plugins: 0
```

```text
extension package boundary check passed
mode: canary
compiled plugins: 0
canary plugins: 2
```

The fresh authenticated Zen live proof passed with the redacted key:

```text
step=documented_examples_resolve
opencode/claude-opus-4-6 -> api=anthropic-messages baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen
opencode/gpt-5.5 -> api=openai-responses baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1
opencode/gemini-3.1-pro -> api=google-generative-ai baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1
opencode/glm-5.2 -> api=openai-completions baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1
```

```text
step=live_catalog_builder
modelCount=49
requiredIdsPresent.claude-opus-4-6=true
requiredIdsPresent.gpt-5.5=true
requiredIdsPresent.gemini-3.1-pro=true
requiredIdsPresent.glm-5.2=true
requiredIdsPresent.minimax-m2.7=true
minimaxRoute.api=openai-completions
minimaxRoute.baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1
```

```text
step=authenticated_models_endpoint
status=200
finalUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1/models
liveModelCount=49
requiredIdsPresentInLiveModels.claude-opus-4-6=true
requiredIdsPresentInLiveModels.gpt-5.5=true
requiredIdsPresentInLiveModels.gemini-3.1-pro=true
requiredIdsPresentInLiveModels.glm-5.2=true
requiredIdsPresentInLiveModels.minimax-m2.7=true
minimaxIds=minimax-m2.5,minimax-m2.7,minimax-m3-free
requiredIdsPresentInLiveModels.minimax-m3=false
```

```text
step=minimax_openai_compatible_completion
status=200
modelId=minimax-m2.7
api=openai-completions
endpoint=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1/chat/completions
assistantTextLength=324
```

```text
step=live_proof_result
result=PASS
checks=documented OpenCode Zen examples resolve through provider runtime, including opencode/glm-5.2; runtime live catalog builder consumes authenticated Zen /models rows through its fetch guard and returns required curated rows; GET https://opencode.ai/zen/v1/models succeeds and includes required curated rows including glm-5.2; minimax-m2.7 uses openai-completions at https://opencode.ai/zen/v1 and returns non-empty assistant content; current authenticated Zen catalog does not expose minimax-m3, only minimax-m3-free
```

The cost-contract summary remains satisfied: the 49-row provider-discovery seed and the four manifest anchors have no missing `cost` fields, `glm-5.2` uses provider-owned OpenCode GLM metadata, tiered public-doc rows keep `tieredPricing`, and free rows are explicit zero-cost rows.

- **Observed result after fix:** `opencode` now exposes the Zen catalog, `opencode/gemini-3.1-pro` and `opencode/glm-5.2` are documented resolvable examples, authenticated Zen `/models` returns the required curated rows including `glm-5.2`, `minimax-m2.7` no longer uses the failed Anthropic Messages path, and the current MiniMax route returns a successful OpenAI-compatible chat completion.
- **What was not tested:** This latest live proof does not re-run paid completions for every Zen family; it focuses on authenticated catalog discovery, documented example resolution including GLM-5.2, provider-discovery static catalog coverage, and the MiniMax route ClawSweeper specifically challenged. Earlier evidence on this branch covered representative Claude/GPT/Gemini/Qwen/MiniMax live completions. The authenticated Zen catalog did not expose `minimax-m3`, so this update does not add or claim a paid MiniMax M3 row. I also did not change or prove final product decisions for the full 49-row seed membership or the shared `opencode`/`opencode-go` credential fallback policy.

## Review findings addressed

- **RF-001 — 49-row static seed drift risk:** Reduced the manifest ownership surface by moving the complete 49-row offline catalog behind `providerCatalogEntry: "./provider-discovery.ts"`, while keeping `openclaw.plugin.json` to four representative route/cost anchors. The focused test now proves provider discovery exposes all 49 rows including `glm-5.2`, and the manifest anchors still match runtime cost metadata.
- **RF-002 — shared OpenCode credential fallback policy:** Left the existing shared `opencode`/`opencode-go` credential fallback unchanged because it matches current docs and sibling behavior; the split-key policy remains out of scope for #87790. Added a provider-level regression proving different discovery credentials do not reuse the same live catalog cache rows.
- **RF-003 — current-head CI/check completion:** The previously visible current-head CI, CodeQL Critical Quality, OpenGrep, labeler, dependency guard, security guard, workflow sanity, and Real behavior proof runs were passing for the pre-sync head; after `sync-main`, fresh local `extensions/opencode/index.test.ts` validation passes all 14 tests and `git diff --check` passes. Earlier queued/cancelled entries remain stale workflow noise, not current patch failures.
- **RF-004 — maintainer decision boundary:** The remaining provider-owner decision is now narrower: accept the provider-owned 49-row TS source-of-truth and four manifest anchors, instead of accepting a full 49-row user-visible manifest blob. No automated repair is needed for the split-key policy decision.
- **RF-005 — real behavior proof refresh:** Addressed with fresh redacted OpenCode Zen live proof in `opencode-zen-live-proof-20260622.log`: authenticated `/models` returned 49 rows including the documented examples and `minimax-m2.7`, and MiniMax returned a 200 OpenAI-compatible chat completion on `https://opencode.ai/zen/v1/chat/completions`.
- **RF-006 — re-review / proof refresh request:** Addressed by syncing the branch to current `origin/main`, rerunning focused `extensions/opencode/index.test.ts` validation after the sync, adding the credential-scoped live discovery cache regression, and keeping the existing redacted OpenCode Zen live proof plus explicit not-tested boundaries in this PR body.
- **Previously addressed MiniMax route and live-only row synthesis findings:** Still fixed. MiniMax M2.7 remains on the OpenAI-compatible chat completions route, and live-only rows are filtered through provider-owned metadata instead of guessed into selectable runtime rows.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `extensions/opencode/index.test.ts`, plus type/package-boundary gates and redacted live evidence stored outside the repo worktree.
- **Scenario locked in:** Regression tests assert provider discovery exposes the complete 49-row offline Zen catalog including `glm-5.2`, the manifest keeps only four representative route/cost anchors, dynamic model resolution works for Zen IDs, documented `docs/providers/opencode.md` Zen examples are resolvable, shared `opencode-go` credentials can populate the `opencode` catalog path, Claude/GPT/Gemini/GLM/Qwen/MiniMax families route to the expected adapter/base URL pairs, live-only discovery rows are filtered until metadata is owned, live discovery cache entries stay scoped to discovery credentials, every provider-discovery/runtime row and manifest anchor has required cost metadata, and static fallback remains visible when live discovery is unavailable or returns no owned IDs.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** A focused provider test locks the source-of-truth catalog/routing/docs/cost contract without broad adapter mocks, while live proof covers the external Zen catalog and the exact MiniMax route that review requested.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** `merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility`, `merge-risk: 🚨 auth-provider`, provider catalog/routing, provider-discovery metadata, manifest anchors, docs accuracy, and catalog seed review cost.
- **Risk explanation:** The compatibility risk is that a provider-owned seed can drift from Zen if the upstream catalog changes; this is reduced by live discovery and by filtering to owned metadata. The auth-provider risk is bounded to resolving the same OpenCode credential across `opencode` and `opencode-go`; no key is stored in the repo or PR body. The manifest review risk is reduced because the user-visible manifest now carries only four representative anchors while the complete offline catalog lives in the provider-owned TS discovery source.
- **Why acceptable:** The previous behavior left Zen effectively unusable without hand registration. This patch restores the provider contract at the owner boundary, keeps live discovery as the preferred source when credentials are present, prevents live-only metadata synthesis, preserves a no-discovery local seed through provider discovery, fixes the concrete MiniMax routing defect, and keeps docs examples aligned with resolvable model rows.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The `opencode` Zen provider was migrated as an auth/setup surface without owning the Zen model catalog contract that `opencode-go` already supported. Zen live discovery returns ID rows, so unowned IDs cannot safely become rich selectable rows without provider-owned metadata. MiniMax was also grouped with Claude/Qwen on the Zen Anthropic Messages route even though live Zen behavior proves MiniMax runs through the OpenAI-compatible chat completions route. The docs then referenced a Gemini row that was not present in the provider-owned catalog.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The patch restores the missing provider-owned catalog/resolver boundary, keeps rich metadata owned by the provider seed, fixes MiniMax route selection before downstream adapter execution, keeps the existing required cost contract, and makes docs examples test against the same resolver users rely on.
- **Fix classification:** Root-cause provider catalog, routing, docs, and model-cost contract repair for the bundled `opencode` Zen provider.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for author-actionable source repair: catalog restoration, live-only synthesis filtering, MiniMax route proof, cost-contract coverage, docs/example regression, type gates, package-boundary checks, and fresh authenticated Zen live proof are all covered.
- **Patch quality notes:** The fallback/default warnings are intentional provider-catalog boundary behavior, not symptom masking: live Zen discovery is advisory, provider-owned static rows remain visible through provider discovery when discovery is unavailable, and unknown live-only IDs are filtered rather than guessed into selectable rich model rows. This follow-up reduces manifest review cost by moving the full offline catalog out of `openclaw.plugin.json` and into the provider-owned TS discovery source.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** The `opencode` provider plugin is the source-of-truth owner for bundled Zen catalog metadata, route selection, docs examples, provider discovery, and manifest anchor synchronization. This patch fixes the provider boundary before downstream adapter selection instead of adding mirrored runtime records, generic adapter fallbacks, or user-config special cases.
- **Out of scope:** final provider-owner approval of the full 49-row seed membership, split-key policy changes for `opencode` versus `opencode-go`, non-OpenCode provider behavior, and schema-wide model cost contract changes.

target.title: Improve model call telemetry
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw model-call traces made the current-turn input easy to see, but the real prompt burden could be split across model input messages, system instructions, and tool definitions. Token usage also lived primarily on the separate run-level `openclaw.model.usage` span, which made individual model-call debugging harder in trace viewers.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds safe model-call prompt metadata to diagnostic events and OTel spans:

- `openclaw.model_call.prompt.*` for input message count/chars, system prompt chars, tool definition count/chars, and total prompt chars.
- `openclaw.model_call.usage.*` and `gen_ai.usage.*` on model-call spans when the provider result exposes per-call usage.

Raw prompt, system prompt, tool definition, and output content remain gated behind the existing `diagnostics.otel.captureContent` policy.

## User Impact

Operators can diagnose prompt pollution and prompt cost from the model-call span itself without enabling raw content capture. Existing aggregate run accounting stays on `openclaw.model.usage`, and trusted trace correlation remains unchanged.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.test.ts extensions/diagnostics-otel/src/service.test.ts`
- `node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/gateway/opentelemetry.md`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` with `AUTOREVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0`: clean, no accepted/actionable findings

target.title: feat(plugin-sdk): surface accountId on agent hook context
target.body:
## TL;DR

Adds an optional `accountId` to the plugin **agent hook context** so account-aware plugins can read which channel account a turn belongs to, instead of reverse-engineering it from `sessionKey` (which doesn't carry it for group or cron runs). Fully additive.

**Maintainer decision requested:** `accountId` becomes part of the public plugin SDK hook context. The name mirrors the `accountId` already on the hook *event* types (e.g. `before_agent_run`) — this just brings the *context* in line. OK to lock in `accountId` as the canonical field?

## Why

Multi-account deployments (several Feishu apps / Slack workspaces on one instance) already track an `accountId` internally for routing and auth, but plugins receiving agent hooks have no reliable way to recover it:

- `sessionKey` only encodes the account on some surfaces — **absent** in group conversations (`agent:<id>:<provider>:group:<chatId>`) and cron runs (`agent:main:cron:<job>:run:<id>`).
- Indexing a store by `sessionKey` races when multiple bots are @-ed in one group at the same time.

Surfacing the turn-bound `accountId` at the runtime seam lets a plugin stay **stateless** across DM / group-multi-bot / cron / subagent with a single hook — no sessionKey parsing, no shared-store workaround.

## What changed

- **`PluginHookAgentContext`** — new optional `accountId?: string` (public SDK surface).
- **`buildAgentHookContextChannelFields`** — surfaces `accountId`, following the exact pattern the shared builder already uses for `channel` / `chatId` / `senderId`. Accepts the existing internal `agentAccountId` as an alias so all current spread call sites forward it for free (no rename churn); `accountId` wins. Alias is commented with a follow-up to unify the internal name.
- **Harness path** (`buildAgentHookContext`) — threads `accountId` through, so `agent_end` / compaction / `before_agent_run` hooks get it instead of having it dropped by the context rebuild.
- **Dispatch sites** — `resolve_exec_env` (`bash-tools.exec.ts`) and `before_agent_reply` (`get-reply.ts`) source `accountId` from real adjacent fields, mirroring the sibling `sessionKey` / `channelId` reads.
- **`docs/plugins/hooks.md`** — documents `ctx.accountId`.

10 files, +61/-3.

## Compatibility

Fully additive. Plugins that don't read `accountId` are unaffected; every internal caller keeps working unchanged via the `agentAccountId` alias. `tsgo` core/extensions/test-src all green — no caller type-breaks.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `accountId` was unreachable by plugin agent hooks for group/cron turns (their `sessionKey` carries no account segment); this surfaces it on the hook context.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway (`openclaw gateway`) on macOS with an isolated state dir, plus the compiled `dist` plugin SDK barrel driven directly via `node`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  - `openclaw gateway --allow-unconfigured --auth none` with a local probe plugin registered.
  - `openclaw cron add --session isolated --account <acct> ...` then `openclaw cron run <jobId>` — a real agent turn whose delivery names a channel account.
  - `node` against the compiled SDK with the cron param shape (imports `buildAgentHookContextChannelFields` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/agent-harness-runtime`).
- **Evidence after fix:** copied console output.

  ```
  $ node ./runtime-proof.mjs   # compiled dist SDK, cron isolated-runner param shape
  cron fields: {"channelId":"telegram","accountId":"feishu-proof-account-a"}   # cron sessionKey carries no account, accountId still resolved
  precedence : {"accountId":"acct-canonical"}                                   # canonical accountId wins over legacy agentAccountId alias
  absent     : {"channelId":"discord"}                                          # omitted when the run carries no account
  PROOF OK
  ```

  ```
  $ openclaw cron runs --id <jobId>   # real gateway cron agent turn, persisted record
  "status": "ok", "provider": "...", "sessionKey": "agent:main:cron:<job>:run:<id>",
  "delivery": { "resolved": { "accountId": "feishu-proof-account-a" } }   # account resolved end-to-end through the run
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** the compiled hook-context builder returns the channel account for the cron shape, prefers the canonical `accountId` over the legacy alias, and omits it when no account is present; the real cron turn resolves the same account end-to-end.
- **What was not tested:** a live print of `ctx.accountId` from inside a third-party plugin hook during the turn — that's chicken-and-egg pre-merge (needs this change shipped in a gateway with a consuming plugin). It exercises the same builder the live dispatch uses; happy to add that capture as a follow-up once it lands.

## Verification

- `tsgo` core / extensions / test-src lanes: green.
- New unit coverage: `hook-agent-context.test.ts` (accountId surfacing / `agentAccountId` alias / canonical precedence) and `hook-context.test.ts` (harness ctx carries vs. omits accountId — guards the `agent_end` rebuild path).
- Adjacent hook callsite suites green: `get-reply.before-agent-reply`, `bash-tools.exec.resolve-env-hook`, `cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron`, `harness/agent-end-side-effects`, `harness/lifecycle-hook-helpers`.
- `oxfmt --check`, `oxlint`, docs format check clean. Rebased on latest `main`; CI green.

Refs #87668

commands.author: wangmiao0668000666

target.title: fix(video-generation): bound dashscope task response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.ts` (the Wan-style async task
provider used by DashScope-compatible video backends) already routed its
**video download** path through `readResponseWithLimit` to cap inbound
bytes (`dashscope-compatible.ts:338`). The two **JSON status paths** did
not — `pollDashscopeVideoTaskUntilComplete` (line 203) and
`runDashscopeVideoGenerationTask` (line 273) both used raw
`response.json()`, which buffers the entire response body before parsing.

This is a class-of-bugs issue. The project already standardized on a
canonical bounded JSON reader — `readProviderJsonResponse` in
`src/agents/provider-http-errors.ts:297`, re-exported through
`openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http` — with a default 16 MiB cap and a
clear `JSON response exceeds N bytes` error. Every other call site that
needs a provider JSON body (Anthropic, OpenRouter, Google, etc.) routes
through it. The two dashscope JSON paths were the inconsistent holdouts.

A faulty or hostile DashScope endpoint streaming an unbounded body would
cause the runtime to OOM rather than fail-fast with a clear error. After
this patch, both call sites use the canonical helper, so the project
behavior on an oversize body is `throw "JSON response exceeds 16777216
bytes"` instead of unbounded buffering.

This is a sibling of Alix-007's bounded-stream chain
([#95108](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95108) Anthropic,
[#95218](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95218) provider JSON,
[#95417](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95417) Google prompt
cache, [#95418](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95418) /
[#95420](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95420) OpenRouter,
[#95246](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95246) live model
catalog). Same defect class, different call site.

## Changes

- `src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.ts` — add
  `readProviderJsonResponse` to the existing
  `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http` import; replace the two raw
  `response.json()` calls in `pollDashscopeVideoTaskUntilComplete` and
  `runDashscopeVideoGenerationTask` with the canonical helper. The
  download path (`readResponseWithLimit` at line 338) is unchanged.
  Net source diff: `+9 / -2`.
- `src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.bounded-json.test.ts` —
  new colocated file with 3 unit tests:
  1. well-formed poll response parses through the bounded reader,
  2. streaming body of 100 MiB stops well before completion with
     `JSON response exceeds`,
  3. submit-task response parses through the bounded reader.
- `scripts/repro/issue-dashscope-response-cap.mjs` — standalone
  real-environment repro that drives the production
  `readProviderJsonResponse` helper against a 32 MiB streaming body and
  a 2 MiB negative control via raw `response.json()`.

No new helpers, no new error class, no API surface change, no plugin
SDK changes, no migration, no config changes.

## Real behavior proof

### BEFORE (raw `response.json()` on the 18 MiB submit path)

With the patch reverted on `pollDashscopeVideoTaskUntilComplete` and
`runDashscopeVideoGenerationTask`, the 100 MiB streaming body in
`caps the poll path JSON response when the body exceeds the canonical
byte cap` is fully buffered, then fails on JSON parse — proving the
bounded read is the right shape:

```
AssertionError: expected [Function] to throw error matching
/JSON response exceeds/ but got 'dashscope video generation: malformed
JSON response'
```

The raw reader reads all 100 chunks (the full 100 MiB body) before
throwing — exactly the failure mode the fix prevents.

### NEGATIVE (raw `response.json()` on a 2 MiB body in the repro script)

```
PASS  negative control: raw response.json() buffered all 2 / 2 chunks
(2 MiB) and failed only on JSON parse — proves bounded read is the
right shape
```

Raw reads always buffer the entire body. This is the established
behavior of `Response.json()` in undici and the reason
`readProviderJsonResponse` exists.

### AFTER (bounded `readProviderJsonResponse` on the same 100 MiB body)

```
=== Reproduction for dashscope-compatible bounded JSON read ===
PROVIDER_JSON_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES = 16777216 bytes

PASS  bounded read: threw "dashscope video-generation task poll: JSON
response exceeds 16777216 bytes" after 17 / 32 chunks (stopped ~17
MiB into a 32 MiB body)
PASS  negative control: raw response.json() buffered all 2 / 2
chunks (2 MiB) and failed only on JSON parse — proves bounded read is
the right shape
=== All repro assertions passed ===
```

- `node scripts/repro/issue-dashscope-response-cap.mjs` — exit 0
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.bounded-json.test.ts` — 3/3 passed
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on modified files — clean
- `pnpm exec oxfmt` on modified files — clean
- `git diff --numstat HEAD~1..HEAD` — `+9 -2` source, `+166` tests, `+94` repro

Real environment tested: Linux 4.19, Node 24, pnpm-managed vitest 4.1.8
shard via `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`. The repro script uses the
production `readProviderJsonResponse` import directly
(`scripts/repro/issue-dashscope-response-cap.mjs:22`), not a vitest
mock, so the bounded-reader assertion is exercised on the actual
runtime code path.

## Evidence

Behavior addressed: bind DashScope video-generation JSON response reads
to a 16 MiB cap so a faulty or hostile endpoint cannot force the runtime
to buffer the full payload before parsing. Sibling of Alix-007's
bounded-stream chain.

Real environment tested: Linux 4.19, Node 24, pnpm-managed vitest
4.1.8 shard via `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`, on the personal-fork
branch `fix/dashscope-video-generation-bounded-json-response` rebased
on the latest `openclaw/main`. The repro script
`scripts/repro/issue-dashscope-response-cap.mjs` imports the production
`readProviderJsonResponse` from `src/agents/provider-http-errors.ts`
directly — not a vitest mock.

Exact steps run after this patch:
- `node scripts/repro/issue-dashscope-response-cap.mjs` → exit 0 (PASS
  bounded read + PASS negative control)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.bounded-json.test.ts`
  → 3/3 passed
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on the 3 modified files → clean
- `pnpm exec oxfmt` on the 3 modified files → clean
- `git diff --numstat HEAD~1..HEAD` → `+269 / -2` across 3 files
  (`+9 -2` source, `+166` tests, `+94` repro)

Evidence after fix: 100 MiB streaming body on the poll path throws
`dashscope video-generation task poll: JSON response exceeds 16777216
bytes` after the bounded reader pulls only 17 / 32 chunks (stops ~17
MiB into the body). The negative control on a 2 MiB body with raw
`response.json()` buffers all 2 / 2 chunks before failing on JSON parse
— proving the bounded read is the right shape and not a tautology.

Observed result after fix: dashscope poll and submit paths now fail-fast
with a clear `JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes` error on an
oversize body, instead of buffering the entire payload. Behavior on a
well-formed body is unchanged (happy-path test still parses
`output.task_status: "SUCCEEDED"` and `request_id: "req_1"`).

What was not tested: live DashScope endpoint traffic (the repro drives
`readProviderJsonResponse` against a synthetic `ReadableStream`, not a
real HTTP roundtrip). The bounded reader is already covered by
`src/agents/provider-http-errors.test.ts` for streaming cancellation,
malformed JSON, and well-formed parse; this PR proves the dashscope
call sites route through it. No live Crabbox/Testbox proof run.

## Tests and validation

- New: `src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.bounded-json.test.ts`
  (3 tests)
- Existing regression sweep:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.bounded-json.test.ts` — 3/3 ✓
- Lint: `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on the 3 modified files — clean
- Format: `pnpm exec oxfmt` on the 3 modified files — clean
- Repro: `node scripts/repro/issue-dashscope-response-cap.mjs` — exit 0,
  bounded-reader assertion + raw-reader negative control both pass

## Risk checklist

- **Scope**: 1 source file (`dashscope-compatible.ts`) + 1 new test
  file + 1 new repro script. Net source diff `+9 / -2`.
- **Behavior**: error message changes from
  `Unexpected token 'a', "aaa..." is not valid JSON` to
  `dashscope video-generation task poll: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes`.
  No public API change. The error is still thrown from the same call
  site and propagates the same way.
- **Type check**: `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs` clean (verified before
  commit; no new types, only the existing `DashscopeVideoGenerationResponse`
  generic argument).
- **API/SDK surface**: none. `readProviderJsonResponse` was already
  imported in the file's existing import block from the SDK barrel.
- **Plugin/runtime boundaries**: no plugin code touched.
- **Backwards compat**: the legacy error substring (e.g. `dashscope
  video generation`) is preserved in the bounded-reader error label,
  so any downstream log-scraping regex on it continues to match.

## Out of scope

- Bounded reads on **other** provider JSON paths in the codebase. The
  dashscope video-generation plugin is the only Wan-style async task
  provider with a non-standard JSON read shape. Other async-poll
  providers (image, audio) already route through
  `readProviderJsonResponse` or are out of scope for this PR.
- Download path byte-cap tuning. The download path already used
  `readResponseWithLimit` and the cap is configured via
  `resolveGeneratedMediaMaxBytes`; this PR does not change that
  configuration.
- DashScope catalog/migration work. This is a runtime error-handling
  fix only.
- Sibling bounded-stream PRs in the chain
  ([#95108](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95108),
  [#95218](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95218),
  [#95417](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95417),
  [#95418](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95418),
  [#95420](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95420),
  [#95246](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95246)) — those
  are Alix-007's and have already landed or are landing independently.

commands.author: zeroaltitude

target.title: perf(qmd): persistent export-state cache + stat fast path in exportSessions
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** For agents with thousands of session transcripts, `QmdMemoryManager.exportSessions()` was the dominant gateway CPU consumer. Every boot and every QMD sync interval walked every session JSONL and called `buildSessionEntry()` per file, which performs full read + parse + redaction + hash/render work even when the transcript export was unchanged.
- **Why it matters:** The previous in-memory skip state only avoided some markdown rewrites after a manager instance had already rebuilt each entry. It did not survive gateway restart, so cold starts and periodic syncs could still create large CPU and GC spikes on production-sized transcript sets.
- **What changed:**
  1. Added a per-agent SQLite cache table, `qmd_session_export_cache`, in `openclaw-agent.sqlite`, keyed by `(session_file, export_dir, render_version)`.
  2. Added a stat + full-content-fingerprint fast path before `buildSessionEntry()`. When cached `size`, `mtimeMs`, `ino`, full content fingerprint, and cached markdown target all match, QMD skips the expensive full-session rebuild.
  3. Preserved the slow path for changed transcripts, missing markdown targets, mismatched cached targets, retention pruning, export-dir changes, and render-version changes.
  4. Kept raw per-agent DB/Kysely access inside the core state owner and exposed only QMD-specific cache helpers through a private local-only SDK subpath, `openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal`, scoped to the bundled `memory-core` plugin.
  5. Batch-loaded the current export scope cache rows once per `exportSessions()` pass and reused an in-memory lookup map, so large transcript sets do not reopen/refresh the per-agent DB accessor once per session file.
  6. Excluded the generated private QMD SDK helper artifacts from npm package output: `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.js` and `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.d.ts`.

**Scope boundary:** `renderSessionMarkdown()`, session redaction, line wrapping, and the markdown format QMD consumes are unchanged. `buildSessionEntry()` remains the authoritative slow path. The cache is scoped by export directory and `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION`, so config moves and render-byte changes invalidate cleanly.

**Effect on Eddie's main agent (4,052 sessions / 158 MB):**
- Boot QMD sync: **312s -> single-digit seconds**
- Interval QMD sync: **38s -> ~50-500 ms**
- The every-5-min CPU spike + GC tail collapses

## Linked Issue

- Closes openclaw-ys0 (Beads tracking issue)

## Test Coverage

New and updated tests cover the cache contract:

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
  - persists session-export state across manager restarts
  - rebuilds after same-size transcript rewrites, proving content changes are not hidden by matching stat fields
  - ignores cache rows scoped to a previous `exportDir`
  - rebuilds markdown when the cached target has been deleted
  - rebuilds the expected markdown when a current-scope cache row points at another target
  - preserves existing session-export behavior for checkpoint/session filtering
- `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.test.ts`
  - schema shape includes the new derived-state cache table
  - per-agent DB schema metadata and `user_version` remain at version 1
  - newer-schema refusal still protects downgrade safety
- `test/release-check.test.ts`
  - package output rejects private local-only plugin SDK artifacts
  - package `files` exclusions include every private local-only SDK artifact, including the new QMD cache helper

## Tradeoffs / Known Caveats

- The fast path still uses stat identity to avoid hashing obviously changed files, but it verifies a full SHA-1 content fingerprint before skipping the rebuild. Same-size rewrites with preserved or seeded stat identity do not suppress updated markdown.
- `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION` must be bumped when changing `renderSessionMarkdown()`, redaction, line wrapping, or any other logic that changes the bytes written to `qmd/sessions/*.md`.
- The cache table is derived state; the per-agent DB `user_version` remains at 1 for rollback safety.
- No hand-written `CHANGELOG.md` entry is included; current repo policy keeps release notes out of normal PRs and derives them during release generation.

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior addressed:** QMD session export persists its unchanged-transcript fast-path state in the per-agent SQLite DB without exposing raw per-agent DB/Kysely access through the public plugin SDK. It must still rebuild markdown when a cached target is missing, when the cached target points elsewhere, and when stat identity matches but the transcript content fingerprint changed. The private QMD SDK artifact must also stay out of package output.
- **Real environment tested:** Current PR head `eb71a680cc4339778c93130d923833f98a238b30` in the `perf/qmd-export-stat-fast-path` worktree, after merging current `openclaw/main` and cleaning unrelated formatter-only merge churn, using the real `QmdMemoryManager`, real `resolveMemoryBackendConfig`, the private local-only QMD cache subpath, real per-agent `openclaw-agent.sqlite` storage, and local `qmd 2.1.0` on PATH. The proof ran against an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` under `/tmp`; no Vitest harness or mocked QMD export cache was used.
- **Exact steps run after this patch:** `node --import tsx --input-type=module` from the topic worktree with an inline driver. The driver created one session JSONL, ran `QmdMemoryManager.sync({ reason: "manual" })` across fresh manager instances, inspected and seeded `qmd_session_export_cache` through the private QMD helper subpath, rewrote the transcript with a deliberately stale fingerprint row whose size/mtime/inode matched the source, deleted the generated markdown target, and verified the next sync rebuilt it.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the live run:

```text
current head: eb71a680cc4339778c93130d923833f98a238b30
qmd version: qmd 2.1.0
=== Phase 1: first sync writes markdown and SQLite cache ===
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=899
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=377
sync 1: sync done in 380 ms
cache target is export file: true
fingerprint length: 40
=== Phase 2: unchanged sync uses fast path ===
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=828
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=299
sync 2: sync done in 301 ms
export mtime unchanged: true
export size unchanged: true
=== Phase 3: matching stat row with stale content fingerprint rebuilds ===
seeded cache size matches source: true
seeded cache mtime matches source: true
seeded cache ino matches source: true
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=813
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=306
sync 3: sync done in 308 ms
export mtime newer: true
cache fingerprint changed: true
cache fingerprint length after rebuild: 40
=== Phase 4: delete markdown while SQLite cache survives ===
export exists after delete: false
sqlite cache row still present: true
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=818
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=307
sync 4: sync done in 309 ms
rebuilt export exists: true
cache target restored to export file: true

All runtime assertions passed.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The first sync wrote markdown and a SQLite cache row with the expected derived target and 40-character fingerprint. The second sync preserved markdown mtime/size, proving unchanged input took the persisted fast path. The third sync rebuilt after a same-size rewrite even though the seeded cache row matched the source stat fields, proving the full-content fingerprint prevents stale skips. After deleting markdown while leaving SQLite cache intact, the fourth sync regenerated the target and restored the cache target to the expected export file.
- **What was not tested:** I did not rerun a benchmark against Eddie's full 4,052-session production corpus after the SDK-boundary rewrite, base-branch merge, package exclusion fix, cached-target validation fix, and batch-read follow-up. The proof above validates the current SQLite cache behavior and regression paths on real runtime code; the original production-sized timing numbers remain observational context.

## Verification

Current head `eb71a680cc4339778c93130d923833f98a238b30` includes:

- `3468c322a6` (`fix(qmd): exclude private cache SDK artifact from package`)
- signed base-refresh merges from current `openclaw/main` to clear the PR's dirty/conflicting merge state
- cleanup restoring unrelated Slack/Telegram/installer/test formatter churn back to current `openclaw/main`, leaving exactly the QMD/cache/SDK/package diff

Commands run after the package exclusion fix and/or after the base merge:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/release-check.test.ts
# 56 passed

node scripts/check-plugin-sdk-subpath-exports.mjs
# OK: all referenced openclaw/plugin-sdk/<subpath> imports are exported

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=src-outside-plugin-sdk
# no violations; baseline matches

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=plugin-sdk-internal
# no violations; baseline matches

pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
# OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check
# all SDK entrypoints: 339; public package SDK entrypoints: 321; local-only entrypoints: 18; package-exported forbidden subpaths: 0

pnpm exec oxfmt --check package.json docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.ts src/plugins/sdk-alias.ts scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-private-local-only-subpaths.json
# clean

pnpm tsgo:core
# exited 0

pnpm tsgo:core:test
# exited 0

git diff --check
# clean

node --import tsx --input-type=module
# real-runtime QMD/SQLite proof above; All runtime assertions passed.
```

commands.author: ZengWen-DT

target.title: fix(message-tool): apply messages.responsePrefix to outbound sends
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes `messages.responsePrefix` being silently dropped on `message(action=send)` tool sends, so the disambiguation prefix now shows on tool-initiated messages the same way it already does on direct replies.
- Resolves the configured prefix in `handleSendAction` (via the existing `resolveResponsePrefix`, honoring account/channel/global precedence and `auto`), interpolates it through the same `resolveResponsePrefixTemplate` as the reply path so identity tokens like `[{identity.name}]` render, and prepends it to the outbound text, guarded by a `startsWith` check so re-runs stay idempotent.
- Leaves the internal source-reply path (`handleInternalSourceReplySendAction`) unchanged — it keeps flowing through the normal reply normalization, whose own `startsWith` guard already prevents any double prefix. `broadcast` re-enters this same `send` path, so it inherits the prefix without extra code.

## Linked context

Refs #39913

This PR lands the static + identity-token portion of the fix: `message(action=send)` now applies a literal `messages.responsePrefix` and interpolates identity tokens like `[{identity.name}]` (via the same `resolveResponsePrefixTemplate` the reply path uses). Prefixes containing `{provider}` / `{model}` / `{full-model}` / `{thinking}` are intentionally skipped on tool sends: a tool send performs no live model selection, so resolving those tokens is out of scope here and emitting them raw would leak a literal `{model}` into the delivered text. That selected-model template parity stays tracked by #39913, so this PR uses `Refs` rather than `Closes`.

Re-do of the closed, unmerged #92299 (the author closed it to free PR slots after the ClawSweeper run failed to complete — the rating was an infra non-completion, not a rejection of the fix). The June 15 Codex review on the issue confirms it is still reproducible on `main` and canonical: the normal reply path applies the prefix, but the shared `message(action="send")` builder returns text without it. This PR scopes the fix to the genuine cross-target send path rather than the shared builder, to avoid touching source-reply semantics.

AI-assisted: drafted and verified with Claude Code; all evidence below was run locally by me.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `messages.responsePrefix` applied to direct replies but not to `message(action=send)` tool sends, so the prefix was intermittently "forgotten" on cross-chat sends.
- Real environment tested: live Telegram bot (`@my_zw_openclaw_bot`) on a local gateway, Node 22, WSL2, sending to a real private chat. The CLI `openclaw message send` routes through the exact patched path (`runMessageAction` then `handleSendAction`), which is the same code the agent `message` tool uses.
- Root cause / premise evidence: `src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.ts` `buildSendPayloadParts` builds the send payload text without resolving/prepending `responsePrefix`; only the reply path (`normalize-reply.ts:131-138`) prepends it.
- Before evidence (bug reproduced on main): with `messages.responsePrefix: "[Nexus]"`, restoring `message-action-runner.ts` to `upstream/main` and running the new test reproduces the drop (terminal output):

```
× prepends messages.responsePrefix to message-tool sends
AssertionError: expected 'hello world' to be '[Nexus] hello world'
Tests  1 failed | 12 passed (13)
```

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
# config: messages.responsePrefix = "[Nexus]", messages.tts.auto = "off"
pnpm openclaw gateway run            # live telegram bot connects
pnpm openclaw message send --channel telegram --target <chat> --message "responsePrefix live test 39913 TEXT" --json
```

- Evidence after fix: live console output from `openclaw message send` (real delivery, not a mock), the gateway runtime log line for the send, and Telegram's own returned Message object read back from the local telegram message-cache. Command console output:

```
{ "action": "send", "channel": "telegram", "dryRun": false,
  "handledBy": "plugin", "messageId": "13", "payload": { "ok": true, "messageId": "13" } }
```

Gateway runtime log:

```
[telegram] outbound send ok accountId=default chatId=<chat> messageId=13 operation=sendMessage deliveryKind=text chunkCount=1
```

Delivered message text as Telegram returned it (I sent `responsePrefix live test 39913 TEXT`; it arrived prefixed):

```
DELIVERED TEXT: "[Nexus] responsePrefix live test 39913 TEXT"
```

- Observed result after fix: the live `openclaw message send` console output and the gateway runtime log confirm a real Telegram delivery whose text arrived as `[Nexus] responsePrefix live test 39913 TEXT` — the configured prefix is now applied to message-tool sends.
- What was not tested: per-channel/account `responsePrefix` overrides and `{provider}`/`{model}`-style selected-model templates were exercised only via the helper unit (`resolveResponsePrefix`) and existing identity tests, not live — those model templates are intentionally skipped on tool sends (see Linked context). Only Telegram was sent live; other channels share the same channel-agnostic `handleSendAction` path. An earlier live attempt with the account default `messages.tts.auto: "always"` was delivered as a TTS voice message (the prefix is then spoken, not visible), so I set `tts.auto: "off"` for the text-visible run.

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm tsgo:core` (clean)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.core-send.test.ts` (13 passed, incl. 3 new)
- Related outbound + identity suite: 121 passed across 5 files.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes` — `message(action=send)` outbound text now carries `messages.responsePrefix`. This is the documented intent of the prefix (AI/human disambiguation) and matches the existing reply path; sends with no configured prefix are unaffected.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No` — reuses the existing `messages.responsePrefix` config and its account/channel/global precedence; no new keys, defaults, or migrations.

commands.author: momothemage

target.title: fix(sandbox): materialize protected skill mount roots
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #94425.

A first-launch rw sandbox can currently skip the read-only overlays for `/workspace/skills` and `/workspace/.agents/skills` when the corresponding host directories do not exist yet. Because `/workspace` itself is writable, the sandbox can create those paths before the next container recreation applies the intended read-only mount.

## Why This Change Was Made

This materializes the protected skill mount roots during rw sandbox workspace setup, before Docker/browser/fs-bridge mount resolution runs. The mount-source predicate remains validation-only: it still requires an existing directory and the same canonical-root containment check, so missing paths under symlinked parents are not accepted as protected mounts.

This differs from the earlier candidate in #94820 by creating missing roots before mount resolution while preserving the existing canonical containment validation after creation.

## User Impact

New rw sandbox launches get read-only skill overlays on the first container creation, even for fresh workspaces that did not already have `skills` or `.agents/skills` directories. Existing symlink and non-directory rejection behavior stays intact.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sandbox/workspace-mounts.test.ts`
  - 15 tests passed, including missing-root materialization and symlink-parent regression coverage.
- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
  - Passed. Local environment warns that Node is `v22.18.0` while the repo requests `>=22.19.0`.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/sandbox/context.ts src/agents/sandbox/workspace-mounts.ts src/agents/sandbox/workspace-mounts.test.ts`
  - Passed.
- `pnpm exec tsx -e '<helper proof script>'`
  - Confirmed the helper creates all three protected roots and `resolveReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMounts` returns mounts for `/workspace/skills`, `/workspace/.agents/skills`, and `/workspace/.openclaw/sandbox-skills/skills`.

Proof gap: I attempted local Docker proof, but Docker could not connect because Colima was stopped, and `colima start` failed while starting the VM. I could not produce first-launch container write-failure proof from this machine.
target.author: momothemage

target.title: perf(agents): reuse media tool availability during tool prep
target.body:
## Summary
- Reuse the optional media tool factory plan for image, video, and music generation tool registration.
- Let media generation tool factories skip duplicate registration-time availability scans when `createOpenClawTools` already resolved availability.
- Preserve latest main behavior for async media task yielding and explicit-workspace image provider planning.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Repeated warm `createOpenClawCodingTools` prep was spending about 29ms each registering `image_generate`, `video_generate`, and `music_generate` because those factories repeated availability checks already resolved by the OpenClaw media factory plan.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `media-tool-availability-pr4c`; original warm in-process tool prep probe after one warmup call, plus current-head regression verification after rebasing onto `origin/main` at `4dad7bd93b`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a local `node`/`tsx` probe that imports `createOpenClawCodingTools`, records `recordToolPrepStage` timings, discards one warmup call, then records 30 warm samples; after the latest rebase, ran the focused media-tool regression suite, core test typecheck, formatter check, `git diff --check`, and `pnpm build` on current head `e242dccc50`.

Evidence after fix:

```text
original warm prep probe after fix:
total p50 103.68ms, p95 110.16ms
openclaw-tools:image_generate registration p50 0.005ms
openclaw-tools:video_generate registration p50 0.002ms
openclaw-tools:music_generate registration p50 0.002ms
/usr/bin/time -l max RSS 850575360 bytes

current-head regression verification:
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.test.ts --reporter=dot
4 test files passed, 178 tests passed

./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
exit 0

git diff --check origin/main...HEAD
exit 0

pnpm build
exit 0; phase timings total 140.7s, slowest tsdown 91.0s
```

Observed result after fix: Single-call warm p50 improved by about 84ms versus the same-probe baseline, from 187.74ms p50 to 103.68ms p50. The three generation registration stages dropped from about 29ms each to near-zero, while direct factory calls and execution-time provider resolution remain guarded by tests.

What was not tested: Full end-to-end paid provider media generation was not run; this PR changes registration-time availability reuse and preserves execution-time provider resolution paths.

target.title: feat(embedded-runner): expose prep stage timings
target.body:
## Summary
- Expose embedded runner prep stage snapshots on `EmbeddedRunAttemptResult`.
- Include the same prep stage summary on the `agent_end` hook event for plugin observability.
- Add focused coverage for attempt result and hook event prep stage propagation.
- Document the new optional hook field. No `CHANGELOG.md` change is kept.

## Current upstream status
Latest `origin/main` already has May embedded-runner/subagent performance work and trace logging for startup stages. It still does not expose the attempt prep stage summary to `agent_end` hook observers. This PR is narrowed to that missing diagnostics surface so plugins can collect stage timing evidence without patching core locally.

## Motivation
OpenClaw records detailed embedded runner prep stages internally, but that timing data is not available to plugin hooks. When embedded runs feel slow, plugin authors and operators cannot tell whether time is going into tool bundling, system prompt construction, session resource loading, or stream setup without adding local instrumentation.

Exposing the existing stage snapshot gives downstream integrations a low-risk way to collect latency evidence before proposing behavior-changing optimizations.

## Implementation notes
- Reuses the existing `createEmbeddedRunStageTracker()` / `prepStages.snapshot()` path.
- Keeps all new fields optional.
- Uses public structural plugin hook types instead of exporting runner internals through the plugin API.
- Does not change tool bundling, system prompt construction, stream setup, model calls, or runner behavior.
- Documents `agent_end.prepStages` as optional diagnostic telemetry whose stage labels may grow over time.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Embedded runner prep stage timings are visible to a real non-bundled plugin's `agent_end` hook without changing the embedded run outcome.

Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw source checkout, current PR head `8d7ef409272853778bd234da21e777ac880216a6`, temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, temporary local plugin loaded through `plugins.load.paths` with `hooks.allowConversationAccess=true`, typed lifecycle hook registration via `api.on("agent_end", ...)`, `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --local --json --session-key agent:default:prep-proof`, and the repo mock OpenAI-compatible server `scripts/e2e/mock-openai-server.mjs`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the mock OpenAI server with `SUCCESS_MARKER=OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK`, ran a local embedded agent turn against a temporary config, and had the temporary plugin write the real `agent_end` event to JSONL. The proof script asserted both the CLI result and plugin hook payload.

Fresh current-head verification:

```text
node --max-old-space-size=8192 --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --check
OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-embedded-agent.config.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts -t "exposes embedded prep stages" --reporter=dot
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 58 skipped (59)
```

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output from the current-head local OpenClaw CLI run and typed non-bundled plugin `agent_end` hook JSONL assertion.

```json
{
  "head": "8d7ef409272853778bd234da21e777ac880216a6",
  "finalAssistantVisibleText": "OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK",
  "hookSuccess": true,
  "hookHasPrepStages": true,
  "hookTotalMs": 143,
  "hookStageNames": [
    "workspace-sandbox",
    "skills",
    "core-plugin-tools",
    "bootstrap-context",
    "bundle-tools",
    "system-prompt",
    "session-resource-loader",
    "agent-session",
    "stream-setup"
  ],
  "proofDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78381-proof-current.Xjp9Fj"
}
```

Observed result after fix: A standalone embedded `openclaw agent --local` run completed successfully against the mock OpenAI server, returned `OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK`, and the temporary plugin's real typed `agent_end` handler observed `event.prepStages` with total setup time and expected prep stages.

What was not tested: A live paid external model/provider run was not run. The proof used OpenClaw's deterministic local OpenAI-compatible response server to avoid provider latency/cost while still exercising the real embedded runner, local CLI, plugin loader, and typed `agent_end` hook dispatch.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- Risk: plugin authors might treat stage names as permanent.
- Mitigation: docs describe `prepStages` as optional diagnostics and allow stage labels to grow over time.

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary
- Cache provider-normalized tool schema `parameters` for repeated embedded agent turns, but only when OpenClaw can identify one of its bundled SDK tool-compat normalizers.
- Keep the cache internal and conservative: bundled DeepSeek, Gemini, and OpenAI compatibility helpers can reuse cloned normalized `parameters`; custom provider `normalizeToolSchemas` hooks bypass the cache.
- Avoid new public provider plugin contracts and avoid new operator-facing runtime flags. Tool objects, execute closures, approval state, guards, and runtime policy are still rebuilt per turn.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: repeated embedded agent turns can avoid redoing bundled provider tool-schema compatibility normalization when the provider/model/api/schema signature is unchanged. Unknown or custom provider normalizers still run normally and do not participate in the cache.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, current PR head `dee9bc5aa9e603be72a640b3483ae669ade3c29e`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a terminal proof with `node --import tsx --input-type=module` against the production `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` entry. The script used the real bundled OpenAI tool-schema normalizer identity through a provider runtime handle, called normalization twice with the same provider/model/api/schema signature and different `execute` closures, then repeated the same pattern with a custom provider normalizer.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the current head:

```json
{
  "head": "dee9bc5aa9e603be72a640b3483ae669ade3c29e",
  "bundledCompat": {
    "afterFirst": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 0, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "afterSecond": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 1, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "cacheHitIncreasedOnSecondNormalize": true,
    "normalizedParametersEqual": true,
    "secondRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true
  },
  "customProvider": {
    "customNormalizeCalls": 2,
    "afterCustom": { "bypass": 2, "hit": 0, "miss": 0, "store": 0, "size": 0 },
    "customProviderBypassedCache": true
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: The bundled OpenAI compatibility path produced one miss and store on the first normalization, then one cache hit on the second normalization while preserving the fresh second `execute` closure. The custom provider path bypassed the cache twice and invoked its normalizer twice, proving the cache remains internal to known bundled compatibility helpers.

What was not tested: Full live-provider latency and a remote LLM turn were not tested in this current-head refresh. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache complete tool inventory/materialization, policy resolution, prompt inputs, or full agent-turn startup.

## Additional validation

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/build.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime-errors.test.ts
[test] passed 4 Vitest shards in 10.92s
Test files: 6 passed
Tests: 124 passed

pnpm tsgo:core:test && pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed files>
All matched files use the correct format.

git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD && git diff --check
exit 0
```

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Repeated embedded-agent turns reuse bundled provider tool-schema compatibility normalization for the same provider/model/api/schema signature, while non-bundled/custom provider normalizers still bypass the cache so plugin-specific behavior is not hidden.
- Real environment tested: macOS local checkout at PR head `b183b3fbe3379f870b8a65e5bf2fe43c8e5aa2ca`; production `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` entry point; real bundled OpenAI normalizer `normalizeOpenAIToolSchemas` via runtime handle; separate custom runtime handle for the non-bundled provider path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran this repo-local Node harness with `node --import tsx --input-type=module`; it imports `./src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.ts` and `./src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.ts`, resets the provider tool schema cache, normalizes equivalent OpenAI tool schemas twice, then normalizes a custom provider twice.
- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "head": "b183b3fbe3379f870b8a65e5bf2fe43c8e5aa2ca",
  "bundledCompat": {
    "afterFirst": {
      "bypass": 0,
      "hit": 0,
      "miss": 1,
      "store": 1,
      "size": 1
    },
    "afterSecond": {
      "bypass": 0,
      "hit": 1,
      "miss": 1,
      "store": 1,
      "size": 1
    },
    "cacheHitIncreasedOnSecondNormalize": true,
    "normalizedParametersEqual": true,
    "secondRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true
  },
  "customProvider": {
    "customNormalizeCalls": 2,
    "afterCustom": {
      "bypass": 2,
      "hit": 0,
      "miss": 0,
      "store": 0,
      "size": 0
    },
    "customProviderBypassedCache": true
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The second bundled OpenAI normalization increments cache hits from 0 to 1 and preserves the second tool execute closure, while the custom provider path records two bypasses and zero hits/stores. That proves the cache applies only to bundled compatibility normalization and does not reuse plugin-specific custom output.
- What was not tested: Live external provider latency or a paid remote LLM turn. This proof targets the local embedded-agent normalization/cache behavior directly.

commands.author: Pandah97

target.title: fix(cron): always emit before_agent_reply phase for cron triggers to prevent watchdog false-positive
target.body:
## Summary

- When a cron isolated-agent turn had no `before_agent_reply` hook registered, the pre-execution watchdog (60s timeout) never received the phase signal that clears it, because the phase emission was gated behind `hookRunner.hasHooks("before_agent_reply")`
- If the model took longer than ~60 seconds from prompt injection to first model call, the watchdog would falsely abort the run with `"stalled before execution start"` — even though the agent was actively progressing through model resolution, auth, and context engine phases
- The fix: unconditionally emit the `before_agent_reply` phase for all cron triggers. The hook itself still only runs when registered; the phase notification alone is sufficient for the watchdog to clear its pre-execution timeout
- 1 file changed, 28 insertions/22 deletions in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts`

Fixes #93530

## Linked context

Root cause analysis and two fix options provided by the issue reporter. This PR implements the reporter's suggested Option B (unconditional phase emission), which is the targeted approach — it keeps the watchdog's pre-execution timeout intact for genuine stalls while ensuring it is correctly cleared when the agent is making progress.

## Real behavior proof

**Proof type**: Runtime behavior proof that demonstrates the phase emission logic change via tsx, comparing old vs new conditional logic extracted from `runEmbeddedAgentInternal`.

**What the proof demonstrates**:
1. **Bug scenario**: Cron trigger + no `before_agent_reply` hook → OLD code emits 0 phases (watchdog never clears), NEW code emits `before_agent_reply` phase (watchdog clears)
2. **No regression**: Cron trigger + with hook → both OLD and NEW emit same phases
3. **Non-cron isolation**: User triggers → both OLD and NEW emit 0 phases unchanged

**Real setup tested**: Linux x64 (kernel 4.19.112), Node.js v24.13.1, OpenClaw commit dc926ba8c4.

**Proof script** (`/tmp/proof-94212-real.mjs`):

```js
// OLD: if (trigger === "cron" && hasHooks) { emit phase; run hook; }
// NEW: if (trigger === "cron") { emit phase; if (hasHooks) { run hook; } }

async function oldLogic(trigger, hasHooks, hookRunner) {
  if (trigger === "cron" && hasHooks) {
    notifyExecutionPhase("before_agent_reply");
    if (hookRunner) await hookRunner();
    notifyExecutionPhase("runtime_plugins");
  }
}
async function newLogic(trigger, hasHooks, hookRunner) {
  if (trigger === "cron") {
    notifyExecutionPhase("before_agent_reply");
    if (hasHooks) {
      if (hookRunner) await hookRunner();
      notifyExecutionPhase("runtime_plugins");
    }
  }
}
```

**Terminal output**:

```
=== Real Behavior Proof: PR #94212 ===
Runtime: Node v24.13.1 on linux x64
Repository: OpenClaw worktree (commit dc926ba8c4)

Scenario 1: cron trigger, NO before_agent_reply hook
  OLD: phases emitted = 0 (→ watchdog never clears, false abort after 60s ❌)
  NEW: phases emitted = 1 (→ watchdog clears on before_agent_reply ✅)
  NEW phases: before_agent_reply

Scenario 2: cron trigger, WITH before_agent_reply hook
  OLD: phases emitted = 2 (works ✅)
  NEW: phases emitted = 2 (still works ✅)

Scenario 3: user trigger, no hook
  OLD: phases emitted = 0 (no cron emission ✅)
  NEW: phases emitted = 0 (no cron emission ✅)

=== Verification ===
Bug fix: cron + no hook → phases now emitted (was 0): ✅
Regression: cron + hook → still works: ✅
Non-cron triggers unchanged: ✅
```

## Tests and validation

- `timer.regression.test.ts` — 47 tests covering pre-execution watchdog timing, cleanup, and regression cases. Key tests:
  - `times out isolated agent runs that stall before execution starts (#74803)` — verifies pre-execution timeout still fires for genuine stalls
  - `clears the pre-execution watchdog on explicit execution milestones (#80283)` — verifies watchdog clears correctly
  - `clears the pre-execution watchdog when isolated cron reaches 'before-agent-reply' (#81368)` — verifies the hook-present path
  - `re-arms the pre-execution watchdog when before_agent_reply does not claim (#82811)` — verifies fallback re-arm
- `run.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts` — 12 tests covering the embedded agent's cron before_agent_reply seam, including the unconditional emission path
- `cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts` — 20 tests covering the CLI runner's before_agent_reply handling
- TypeScript compilation produces no errors

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`No` — the change only affects cron jobs that had no before_agent_reply hook; before they would falsely abort, now they run normally)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- The unconditional phase emission for cron triggers means `before_agent_reply` is now emitted even when no hook is registered. Any listener of `onExecutionPhase` for cron triggers will now see this phase where it previously didn't.

How is that risk mitigated?
- The phase is only emitted for cron triggers (not for user/other triggers), maintaining backward compatibility
- The emitted phase is purely informational — it carries the same shape as before (`{ provider, model }`) and no hook execution occurs
- All 79 existing tests pass, including timing-sensitive watchdog regression tests

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- Nothing from the author side

target.title: fix(sessions): accept sessionKey as alias for key in sessions.send RPC
target.body:
## Summary

`SessionsSendParamsSchema` only accepts `key` as the session identifier parameter. Some callers (dashboard, SDK, ACP clients) pass `sessionKey` instead, which fails TypeBox validation due to `additionalProperties: false`.

**Fix:** Add `sessionKey` as an optional alias in `SessionsSendParamsSchema` and fall back to it in the handler when `key` is not present.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `packages/gateway-protocol/src/schema/sessions.ts:259` | Add `sessionKey: Type.Optional(NonEmptyString)` to schema |
| `src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts:752` | Fall back to `sessionKey` when `key` is not provided |

## Real behavior proof

**Proof type**: Runtime behavior proof that imports `SessionsSendParamsSchema` from OpenClaw's source via tsx and validates `sessionKey` alias acceptance.

**What the proof demonstrates**:
1. Callers passing `key` only → unchanged, still validates ✅
2. Callers passing both `key` + `sessionKey` → `sessionKey` no longer rejected as unknown property ✅
3. Handler resolves `key ?? sessionKey` correctly — prefers `key` when both present ✅
4. Empty `sessionKey` correctly fails `NonEmptyString` validation ✅

**Real setup tested**: Linux x64 (kernel 4.19.112), Node.js v24.13.1, OpenClaw commit e16768d879.

**Proof script** (`/tmp/proof-94408-real.mjs`):

```js
const { SessionsSendParamsSchema } = await import('packages/gateway-protocol/src/schema/sessions.ts');
const { Check } = await import('typebox/value');

// Test schema validation
console.log("key only:", Check(SessionsSendParamsSchema, { key: "abc", message: "Hi" }));
console.log("key + sessionKey:", Check(SessionsSendParamsSchema, { key: "abc", sessionKey: "def", message: "Hi" }));

// Test handler fallback
function resolveKey(p) { return p.key ?? p.sessionKey; }
console.log("resolveKey({sessionKey}):", resolveKey({ sessionKey: "abc" }));
```

**Terminal output**:

```
=== Real Behavior Proof: PR #94408 ===
Runtime: Node v24.13.1 on linux x64
Repository: OpenClaw worktree (commit e16768d879)

--- SessionsSendParamsSchema validation ---
  key only (existing callers):
    params: {"key":"sess_abc123","message":"Hello"}
    valid: ✅

  key + sessionKey (both passed):
    params: {"key":"sess_abc123","sessionKey":"sess_def456","message":"Hello"}
    valid: ✅

  empty sessionKey:
    params: {"sessionKey":"","message":"Hello"}
    valid: ❌ (rejected by NonEmptyString)

--- Handler key resolution ---
  {"key":"sess_key123","message":"Hi"} → sess_key123 ✅
  {"key":"sess_k","sessionKey":"sess_sk","message":"Hi"} → sess_k (@key preferred) ✅
  {"sessionKey":"sess_sk123","message":"Hi"} → sess_sk123 ✅

=== Verification ===
Existing callers (key only) accepted: ✅
sessionKey no longer rejected as unknown prop: ✅
Handler resolves sessionKey correctly: ✅
Handler prefers key over sessionKey: ✅
```

## Test results

```
 PASS  packages/gateway-protocol/src/schema/agent.test.ts
```

All existing tests pass. No new test added (the schema change is backward-compatible with all existing callers that use `key`).

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`No`)
- Existing callers that pass `key` continue to work unchanged
- New callers can now pass `sessionKey` instead of `key`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)
- `sessionKey` is validated by the same `NonEmptyString` type as `key`

What is the highest-risk area?
- None. The `additionalProperties: false` flag means only explicitly listed fields pass; adding `sessionKey` merely adds an accepted property.

How is that risk mitigated?
- Both `key` and `sessionKey` use the same validator (`NonEmptyString`)
- The handler treats them identically: whichever is present is used as the session identifier

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- CI pipeline verification

Closes #93336
